critique:
Critique of planning/250620c_glossary_generate_more_timeout_mitigation.md
============================================================================

Executive summary  
-----------------
The document is thoughtful and broadly on-track: it squarely targets the 504 bottleneck, keeps Claude Sonnet quality, and incrementally improves UX.  Most architectural choices match the existing codebase patterns (Nunjucks+Zod, frontend-driven queue, Supabase JSONB).  However there are several blind spots that could undermine the goal or cause follow-up re-work:

• Root-cause vs symptom – we still send the **entire document** on every call; the plan only caps the number of entities returned, not the prompt length.  
• Concurrency / idempotency – “Generate more” from multiple tabs or fast clicks can create duplicates or race conditions.  
• Scalability of JSON-blob storage – one row per glossary will hit 8 MB Postgres limit long before “hundreds of entities”.  
• Position detection cost – repeated DOM scanning in the browser will become O(N×M).  
• Prompt/response contracts – not yet specified with Zod; no guaranteed `more_entities_available` flag.  
• Observability / token budgeting – we will not know which documents still cause timeouts until after they fail.

Below are detailed findings and suggestions, grouped by impact.

1. High-impact technical risks  
------------------------------

1.1 Document length still sent in full  
• Each “Load More” keeps “Preserve full document context” ⇒ same token cost, so the only savings are *generation* tokens, not *prompt* tokens.  For a 20-page scientific paper, the prompt can still exceed 16 K tokens → Sonnet latency ~40 s even for 10 entities.  
• Consider a hybrid: include the **full abstract/introduction + snippets around each existing entity + 4 K token budget headroom** instead of the entire doc.  You can compute these snippets once and cache them in Supabase (`document_context_excerpt`).

1.2 Duplicate / out-of-order entities under concurrency  
• If a user quickly presses “Load More” twice, two requests race, each unaware of the other’s soon-to-exist entities.  
• Mitigation: assign a **deterministic hash key** (e.g. `slug(name)`) server-side and use `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` when upserting entities (requires table approach, see 2.2).  Alternatively lock the button until the first call returns.

1.3 Storage ceiling of JSONB blob  
• Postgres row limit is 268 MB compressed, but Vercel / Supabase network timeout and our API memory will bottleneck earlier.  More importantly **incremental updates overwrite the entire blob → race conditions and large write bandwidth**.  
• Strongly recommend moving to `glossary_entities` table:

```
document_id  uuid  → documents.id
slug         text  pk
name         text
aliases      text[]
brief        text
long         text
position     int   nullable
created_at   timestamptz default now()
```

The planning doc does mention an evaluation but treats it as optional – I would move it to Stage 1 because later migration will be painful.

1.4 DOM scanning cost  
`components/unified-left-pane.tsx:findFirstOccurrence` currently walks the rendered document on the client.  With 300 entities this becomes noticeable.  After adopting the table approach, compute `position` **once on the server** with a simple string index (`html.indexOf(entity.regex)`) ­– you already have the entire HTML in the database.

2. Architectural & design comments  
----------------------------------

2.1 API contract  
• Define a Zod response schema in `lib/prompts/templates/glossary.ts`:

```
export const glossaryEntitySchema = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1),
  aliases: z.array(z.string()).optional(),
  brief_explanation: z.string().min(10),
  long_explanation: z.string().min(10).optional(),
  score_difficulty: z.number().min(0).max(1).optional(),
  score_centrality: z.number().min(0).max(1).optional(),
})
export const glossaryLLMResponse = z.object({
  entities: z.array(glossaryEntitySchema),
  more_entities_available: z.boolean().optional(),
})
```

• Validate both LLM output and the JSON returned by `POST /api/glossary`.  This protects against prompt drift.

2.2 Prompt inputs  
Sending the **whole existing entity object** back to the LLM wastes tokens.  Only send a deduplicated list of entity names/aliases (`existing_names: ["qPCR","Western blot",…]`) – a few hundred tokens not thousands.  Update the task list accordingly.

2.3 Config vs runtime limits  
Hard-coding `MAX_ENTITY_LIMIT: 100` is brittle; rely instead on an **environment variable** so we can tweak quickly in prod (`NEXT_PUBLIC_GLOSSARY_MAX_LIMIT`).  Document this in `docs/reference/CONFIGURATION_FLAGS.md`.

2.4 Timeout handling  
The 504 comes from Vercel edge / Next.js default 30 s limit.  Add a **timeout guard** in `app/api/glossary/route.ts` via `AbortController` at 27 s → return 504 early with `retryable: true`.  Frontend can show “Generation still running, click retry”.

3. Frontend concerns  
--------------------

3.1 State management  
The plan says “Track loaded vs available entities”, but not where.  If we keep everything in React context, navigation between documents may leak state.  Use **Zustand store** or React-Query cache keyed by `documentId`.

3.2 UX for very long glossaries  
Consider replacing a single “Load More” button with **infinite scroll** or **progressive disclosure (“show next 20” chunk)** once entity count > 100.

3.3 Error cases  
• Network failure: re-queue the same “generate more” request with exponential back-off.  
• LLM returns duplicate names: highlight in UI with ⚠️ icon and allow user to delete duplicates.

4. Prompt engineering details  
-----------------------------

4.1 Stopping criterion  
LLMs often ignore “up to N entities”; add an explicit rule: “If fewer than {{max_entities}} pertinent entities remain, STOP and output `"more_entities_available": false"`.”  Verify with unit tests (mocked LLM responses).

4.2 Injection & template safety  
Because we interpolate raw entity names into `{% if existing_entities %}` block, escape with `| dump` filter or pass as JSON string.

4.3 Token optimisation  
Place `existing_names` AFTER the main instructions in the prompt – Claude pays more attention to early tokens, reducing chance of repetition.

5. Observability & cost monitoring  
----------------------------------

Add Pino child logger in route.ts:

```
const timer = createTimer()
log.info({docId, limit, existingCount}, 'Glossary generation started')
… call LLM …
log.info({...timer(), newEntities: res.entities.length}, 'Glossary generation finished')
```

Track total input+output tokens via model telemetry (Anthropic API returns it).  Persist in `glossary_entities_calls` table for later optimisation.

6. Testing strategy improvements  
--------------------------------

• Unit tests – focus on prompt assembly and Zod validation.  
• Integration – mock Anthropic with deterministic fixtures (see `lib/testing/anthropic-mock.ts`) and assert dedup + DB writes.  
• E2E – Playwright script that uploads a 25-page PDF and verifies ≤ 5 s response for first 20 entities.

7. Documentation gaps  
---------------------

Update `docs/reference/TOOL_GLOSSARY.md` with:

• API field list  
• Behaviour when `more_entities_available=false`  
• Concurrency rules (button disabled until call returns)  
• Storage schema (table vs JSONB decision + migration plan)

8. Suggested re-ordering of stages  
----------------------------------

1. Decide storage model (JSONB vs table) – this is foundational  
2. Add Zod schemas & prompt changes (capping & duplicate exclusion)  
3. Implement route.ts changes + unit tests  
4. Frontend “Load More” with disabled-while-loading pattern  
5. Observability & timeout guard  
6. Position tracking server-side  
7. Advanced features (scoring, user-requested terms, long explanations)

By locking the storage & API contract first, later stages become simpler and require fewer migrations.

Closing note  
------------  
Overall the plan is salvageable with moderate adjustments.  The most critical change is reducing prompt length and adopting an append-only entity table; without these, we risk hitting the same 504s and introducing data consistency problems.  Address the highlighted points and the feature should scale to very large documents while preserving Claude Sonnet quality.
